1	\chapter{Relative Picard sheaf --- \texttt{Scheme.Modules.tensorObj} substrate (A.1.c.SubT)}
2	\label{chap:Picard_TensorObjSubstrate}
3	
4	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
5	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
6	
7	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
8	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
9	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
10	\[
11	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
12	  \qquad
13	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
14	\]
15	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
16	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
17	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
18	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
19	
20	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
21	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
22	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
23	% groups)
24	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
25	% SOURCE QUOTE:
26	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
27	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
28	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
29	% abelian groups defined by the formula
30	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
31	% \end{dfn}
32	%  [\dots]
33	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
34	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
35	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
36	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
37	% topologies  by
38	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
39	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
40	%  \end{dfn}"
41	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
42	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
43	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
44	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
45	homomorphisms.}
46	
47	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
48	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
49	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
50	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
51	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
52	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
53	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
54	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
55	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
56	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
57	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
58	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
59	structure. This is the content of
60	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
61	
62	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
63	infrastructural, and --- crucially --- much smaller than a full
64	monoidal-category construction. The group law lives on \emph{isomorphism
65	classes} of line bundles: each group axiom is a \emph{proposition} asserting
66	the existence of an isomorphism (\(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\,\cdots \cong \cdots)\)),
67	and a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it. In
68	particular the coherence data of a monoidal category --- the pentagon,
69	triangle and hexagon identities, and a fortiori any closed structure --- is
70	\emph{never consumed} by a group law on iso-classes. What the law genuinely
71	requires on the carrier is therefore only:
72	\begin{enumerate}
73	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
74	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
75	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), well-defined on
76	    isomorphism classes;
77	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
78	    for \(\otimes\);
79	  \item the \(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate
80	    (\(\exists N,\ M \otimes_X N \cong \mathcal{O}_X\)) together with the three
81	    monoidal coherence isomorphisms of \(\otimes_X\): associativity
82	    \((M \otimes N) \otimes P \cong M \otimes (N \otimes P)\), unit
83	    \(\mathcal{O}_X \otimes M \cong M\) (and on the right), and commutativity
84	    \(M \otimes N \cong N \otimes M\). The inverse is carried by the
85	    predicate, not by a separate lemma.
86	\end{enumerate}
87	At Mathlib's pinned commit one might be tempted to obtain (3) by instantiating a
88	\emph{full} symmetric monoidal category on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) and
89	reading the three isomorphisms off it. Two Mathlib facts make such a route
90	conceivable. First, the category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves
91	of modules over a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) is already a (symmetric)
92	monoidal category in Mathlib (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\)),
93	with the sectionwise relative tensor \((M \otimes_R N)(U) = M(U) \otimes_{R(U)}
94	N(U)\); this applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf
95	\(R = \mathcal{O}_X\). Second, Mathlib's localization-of-monoidal-categories API
96	(\(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\)) takes a
97	monoidal category \(C\), a localization functor \(L : C \to D\) at a morphism
98	property \(W\), and an instance \(W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and produces a
99	\emph{full} monoidal-category structure on the localization \(D\); sheafification
100	of presheaves of modules is the localization \(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the
101	locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\), so a full monoidal structure on
102	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) would follow once \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) were
103	supplied.
104	
105	This full-monoidal-category route is, however, \emph{not} the goal, and is not
106	pursued. The goal is the commutative \emph{group} on locally-trivial iso-classes
107	(à la \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\)), and that group law consumes only propositions, so
108	it never needs a coherent monoidal category --- neither the
109	pentagon/triangle/hexagon identities nor the \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance
110	that the \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) API requires. What it needs is exactly the
111	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms of (3), and these are produced
112	directly: the associator by gluing canonical local isomorphisms
113	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}, via the single linchpin
114	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), the unitors by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\)
115	pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso}), and the braiding likewise
116	(\cref{lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). With those three existence facts the group law
117	assembles the commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes whose units group is the
118	Picard group (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}), exactly as Mathlib builds
119	\(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\) as
120	\(\mathrm{Units}(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R))\) over a fixed ring. The
121	\(\otimes\)-invertibility predicate (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible},
122	Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\) idiom) carries each inverse existentially.
123	The whole \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) / \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / stalk
124	apparatus is therefore vestigial for the group law and off the critical path.
125	
126	\section{Mathlib API survey}
127	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
128	
129	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
130	(\texttt{b80f227}) were originally assembled into a single \emph{realization route}
131	(route~(e)): obtaining a full monoidal structure on the substrate by instantiating
132	Mathlib's abstract localization-of-monoidal-categories API rather than
133	hand-assembling the associator. That route is \emph{superseded}. The group law on
134	iso-classes never consumes a coherent monoidal category, and the associator is
135	obtained by direct gluing through \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, so the
136	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) instance on which route~(e) hinges is never required.
137	The survey below is retained as a Mathlib-API reference and to record why the route
138	is vestigial; the four Mathlib facts are all verified against the on-disk Mathlib
139	source.
140	
141	\paragraph{(i) The presheaf-of-modules monoidal category.} Mathlib's
142	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Presheaf.Monoidal} constructs a
143	monoidal-category instance
144	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory}\) on
145	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,
146	\mathtt{RingCat})\), with the sectionwise relative tensor
147	\[
148	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
149	\]

151	% `PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategory`, in
152	% `Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean` (L104, L125)
153	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/Algebra/Category/ModuleCat/Presheaf/Monoidal.lean).
154	% SOURCE QUOTE:
155	% "noncomputable instance monoidalCategoryStruct :
156	%     MonoidalCategoryStruct (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where
157	%   tensorObj := tensorObj
158	%   whiskerLeft _ _ _ g := tensorHom (𝟙 _) g
159	%   whiskerRight f _ := tensorHom f (𝟙 _)
160	%   ...
161	%   tensorUnit := unit _
162	%   associator M₁ M₂ M₃ := isoMk (fun _ ↦ α_ _ _ _) ...
163	% noncomputable instance monoidalCategory :
164	%     MonoidalCategory (PresheafOfModules.{u} (R ⋙ forget₂ _ _)) where ..."
165	together with associator, left/right unitors and braiding making it symmetric
166	monoidal. The base presheaf of rings \(R \circ \mathtt{forget}_2\,
167	\mathtt{CommRingCat}\,\mathtt{RingCat}\) is exactly the project's
168	\(\mathtt{Sheaf.val}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\) carrier (equal by \(\mathtt{rfl}\)),
169	so this monoidal structure applies verbatim to the \emph{varying} structure sheaf

172	
173	\paragraph{(ii) The localization-of-monoidal-categories API.} Mathlib's
174	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.Basic} provides the class
175	\(\mathtt{MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal}\) and the construction
176	\(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\). The class records exactly
177	the whiskering-stability data:
178	% SOURCE: [Mathlib], `CategoryTheory.MorphismProperty.IsMonoidal` and
179	% `CategoryTheory.Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal`, in
180	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean` (L44, L86, L440)
181	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Localization/Monoidal/Basic.lean).
182	% SOURCE QUOTE:
183	% "class IsMonoidal : Prop extends W.IsMultiplicative where
184	%   whiskerLeft (X : C) {Y₁ Y₂ : C} (g : Y₁ ⟶ Y₂) (hg : W g) : W (X ◁ g)
185	%   whiskerRight {X₁ X₂ : C} (f : X₁ ⟶ X₂) (hf : W f) (Y : C) : W (f ▷ Y)
186	%  ...
187	% def LocalizedMonoidal (L : C ⥤ D) (W : MorphismProperty C)
188	%     [W.IsMonoidal] [L.IsLocalization W] {unit : D} (_ : L.obj (𝟙_ C) ≅ unit) := D

192	Given \([\MonoidalCategory\,C]\), \([W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}]\), a localization
193	functor \(L : C \to D\) with \([L.\mathtt{IsLocalization}\,W]\) and an isomorphism

200	\paragraph{(iii) The proof template for $W$-monoidality.} Mathlib's
201	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW} proves
202	\((J.W\,(A := A)).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) \emph{stalkwise} for presheaves valued in a
203	\emph{fixed} monoidal concrete category \(A\) (i.e.\ \(C^{op} \to A\)), via the
204	conservative-family characterisation \(\mathtt{hP.W\_iff}\),
205	\(\mathtt{Functor.Monoidal.map\_tensor}\) and typeclass inference:

207	% and the `(J.W).IsMonoidal` instance, in
208	% `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean` (L48, L57)
209	% (read from .lake/packages/mathlib/Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Sites/Point/IsMonoidalW.lean).
210	% SOURCE QUOTE:
211	% "lemma ObjectProperty.IsConservativeFamilyOfPoints.isMonoidal_W
212	%     [J.HasSheafCompose (forget A)] :
213	%     (J.W (A := A)).IsMonoidal :=
214	%   .mk' _ (fun f g hf hg ↦ by
215	%     simp only [hP.W_iff (A := A)] at hf hg ⊢

219	This is the \emph{template} for the proof technique used in route~(e), but it does
220	\emph{not} apply directly to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\): modules over a
221	\emph{varying} ring are not of the form \(C^{op} \to A\) for a fixed monoidal
222	\(A\).

224	\paragraph{The gap and route~(e), superseded.} The substrate
225	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\) of this chapter is the
226	sheafification of \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\), and
227	sheafification of presheaves of modules is exactly the localization
228	\(L = \mathtt{sheafification}\) at the locally-bijective class \(W = J.W\). Route~(e)
229	proposed to obtain a \emph{full} monoidal structure on
230	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X = \mathtt{SheafOfModules}\,X.\mathtt{ringCatSheaf}\)
231	by applying \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) (ii) to the already-monoidal category (i),
232	which would require the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (its two
233	whiskering-stability fields \cref{lem:whisker_of_W}, with load-bearing
234	local-injectivity half \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}). This is
235	\emph{not} how the group law is realized. The group law consumes only the
236	\emph{existence} of the three coherence isomorphisms, and the associator is built
237	directly by gluing canonical local isomorphisms through
238	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}); it never invokes
239	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), a coherent monoidal category, or the stalkwise
240	whiskering apparatus. The route~(e) instance is therefore vestigial and off the
241	critical path.

243	The stalk-infrastructure analysis that route~(e) turned on is recorded here for
244	reference only, since that route is superseded. The \emph{stalk module structure}
245	is already present in Mathlib: the file
246	\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Stalk} supplies, for a topological
247	space \(X\), a presheaf of commutative rings \(R\) on \(X\) and a point
248	\(x \in X\), the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-module structure on the stalk
249	\(\mathtt{stalk}\,M.\mathtt{presheaf}\,x\) of a presheaf of modules \(M\),
250	together with the germ--scalar compatibility \(\mathtt{germ\_smul}\); a linearity
251	packaging of the induced stalk map of a morphism was built project-side
252	(\cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}, the \emph{linearity half} of ingredient (d.1)).
253	Route~(e) additionally required a (d.1)-\emph{bridge} between the site-level
254	\(J.W\) and the topological stalkwise-isomorphism criterion on
255	\(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\), and (d.2) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
256	\otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk with the relative module-presheaf tensor; there is
257	moreover no monoidal \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules}\) in Mathlib. Because the group law
258	is realized through the direct-gluing associator (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso},
259	via \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}) and never through
260	\(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), none of this stalk apparatus is on the critical path;
261	it is retained only to document the superseded route.
262	
263	The project's policy on this gap is \emph{project-side}: the substrate is
264	built inside \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (the file
265	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\) is its home)
266	and the consumer instance is closed against it. A parallel Mathlib upstream
267	PR with the same content is welcome but not on the critical path for the
268	project; the consumer is closed against the project-side substrate
269	independently of any upstream timing.

271	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
272	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
273	
274	\begin{definition}
275	
276	\leanok
277	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
278	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
279	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
280	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
281	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
282	  \[
283	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
284	  \]
285	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
286	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
287	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
288	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
289	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
290	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
291	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
292	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
293	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
294	  canonical compatibility
295	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
296	     \;\cong\;
297	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
298	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
299	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
300	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
301	
302	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
303	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
304	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
305	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
306	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
307	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
308	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
309	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
310	\end{definition}

361	\begin{remark}
362	[The full monoidal category on all of $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$ is off-path; the group law consumes only propositions]
363	  \label{rem:scheme_modules_monoidal_off_path}
364	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality,
365	        lem:whisker_of_W}
366	  One could ask for the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
367	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, together with
368	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), \(\alpha\), \(\lambda\), \(\rho\) and \(\beta\), to
369	  assemble into a full symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure on
370	  \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), discharging the pentagon,
371	  triangle and hexagon coherence identities. This is explicitly \emph{off-path}
372	  and \emph{not pursued}. One could in principle obtain it as the output of
373	  \(\mathtt{Localization.Monoidal.LocalizedMonoidal}\) applied to the
374	  already-monoidal presheaf category \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_X\)
375	  and the sheafification localizer \(J.W\), supplying the instance
376	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) (\cref{lem:whisker_of_W}); but the group law never
377	  consumes a coherent monoidal category, so this construction is superseded and not
378	  carried out. The substrate provides only the \emph{existence} of the three
379	  coherence isomorphisms that the group law actually consumes.
380	
381	  Were that superseded route pursued, the instance \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\)
382	  would need \emph{no} closed structure: its
383	  \(\mathtt{whiskerLeft}\) field \(W\,g \Rightarrow W(F \triangleleft g)\) for an
384	  \emph{arbitrary} module \(F\) is the flatness-free stalkwise statement
385	  \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W} (a \(J.W\)-morphism \(g\) is a
386	  stalkwise isomorphism, so \((F \triangleleft g)_x = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes
387	  g_x\) is again an isomorphism for any \(F\)), the same technique Mathlib uses for
388	  fixed-base presheaves in
389	  \(\mathtt{CategoryTheory.Sites.Point.IsMonoidalW}\). It would use neither
390	  \(\mathtt{MonoidalClosed}(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R)\) nor the
391	  sectionwise-flatness hypothesis of \cref{lem:flat_whisker_localizer} (which is
392	  false over a non-affine open and is likewise off the critical path). But this
393	  apparatus is not on the critical path at all: the group law is realized without
394	  it.
395	
396	  Independently of how the monoidal structure is built, the relative Picard
397	  group law of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} is a structure on
398	  \emph{isomorphism classes} of line bundles, and every group axiom there is a
399	  \emph{proposition} of the form \(\mathrm{Nonempty}(\cdots \cong \cdots)\).
400	  Such a proposition is insensitive to which isomorphism witnesses it, so the
401	  coherence data of the monoidal category --- pentagon, triangle, hexagon, and
402	  a fortiori any monoidal-closed structure --- is never \emph{consumed} by the
403	  group law. The group law needs only the existence of the three coherence
404	  isomorphisms \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,%
405	  lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}, supplied directly: the associator by gluing canonical
406	  local isomorphisms through the restriction-compatibility isomorphism
407	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} (\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}), and the
408	  unitors and braiding by the cheap \(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern
409	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}). This mirrors Mathlib's own
410	  Picard group \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}),
411	  whose \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) is transported from
412	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\): there
413	  over a fixed ring, here over iso-classes of the substrate tensor \(\otimes_X\).
414	\end{remark}

416	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
417	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
418	
419	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
420	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
421	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
422	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
423	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section records the
424	\emph{existence-of-isomorphism facts on line bundles} (associativity, unit,
425	commutativity and inverse) that the relative Picard group law consumes. These facts
426	are supplied \emph{directly}: associativity by the direct-gluing associator
427	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso} (through the linchpin
428	\cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}), unit and commutativity by the cheap
429	\(\mathtt{mapIso}\) pattern (\cref{lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso}),
430	and the inverse existentially from \(\otimes\)-invertibility. They are \emph{not}
431	read off any \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure: the route that would derive them from
432	\cref{lem:jw_ismonoidal} (route~(e)) is superseded and off-path. The section also
433	records the closure of \(\otimes\) on the locally-trivial
434	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\) subtype (\cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}) and
435	the interaction with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\). The open-immersion
436	restriction-compatibility isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is on the
437	critical path precisely because it is consumed by the direct-gluing associator
438	\cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}; it is \emph{not} tied to the superseded
439	whiskering-stability lemma \cref{lem:islocallyinjective_whisker_of_W}, which belongs
440	to the off-path route~(e).

442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Sectionwise lax-monoidal structure on \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\)]
446	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_laxmonoidal}
447	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal}
448	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
449	  % NOTE: this CommRingCat-level lax-monoidal instance
450	  % (`PresheafOfModules.restrictScalarsLaxMonoidal`, with helpers
451	  % `restrictScalarsLaxε`, `restrictScalarsLaxμ`) is a self-contained
452	  % supplement. It is NOT used by `lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso`, whose proof
453	  % (open-immersion sectionwise base change) identifies restriction along an
454	  % open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
455	  % needs no lax-monoidal pushforward. Retained for potential reuse; off the
456	  % critical path for the tensor group law.
457	  Let \(\varphi : R \to S\) be a morphism of presheaves of \emph{commutative}
458	  rings on a site (so that both \(R\) and \(S\) factor through
459	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) and the category of presheaves of modules over each
460	  is monoidal). Then the presheaf-of-modules restriction-of-scalars functor
461	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) is lax monoidal.
462	  Consequently, composing with the already-monoidal Mathlib functor
463	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) via
464	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\), the full pushforward
465	  \[
466	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi
467	      \;\;:=\;\;
468	    \mathtt{pushforward}_0\,F\,R \;\circ\; \mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi
469	  \]
470	  is lax monoidal. This lemma is a self-contained
471	  \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-level lax-monoidal supplement: it stands on its own
472	  and is retained for potential reuse, but it is \emph{off the critical path}
473	  for the tensor group law. In particular it is \emph{not} an ingredient of
474	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, whose proof identifies restriction along an
475	  open immersion with base change along a structure-sheaf isomorphism and
476	  consequently needs no lax-monoidal structure on the pushforward.
477	\end{lemma}
478	
479	\begin{proof}
480	  This is a \emph{sectionwise} lift of the existing fact that, for a ring map
481	  \(f : R \to S\), the module restriction-of-scalars functor
482	  \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,f\) is lax monoidal
483	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Monoidal.Adjunction}, where it is
484	  obtained as the \(\mathtt{rightAdjointLaxMonoidal}\) of the
485	  extension/restriction adjunction \(\mathtt{extendRestrictScalarsAdj}\) ---
486	  i.e.\ Mathlib already uses the same mate idiom one level down). The presheaf
487	  monoidal structure is computed sectionwise,
488	  \((M_1 \otimes M_2).\mathrm{obj}\,X = M_1.\mathrm{obj}\,X \otimes M_2.\mathrm{obj}\,X\),
489	  and the presheaf functor \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\,\varphi\) acts
490	  sectionwise as \(\mathtt{ModuleCat.restrictScalars}\,(\varphi.\mathrm{app}\,X)\).
491	  Hence the lax structure maps \(\mu\) (laxity) and \(\varepsilon\) (unit)
492	  assemble from the per-section \(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\) ones, the required
493	  naturality squares being the naturality of the sectionwise maps against the
494	  presheaf restriction morphisms. Packaging these as a
495	  \(\mathtt{Functor.CoreLaxMonoidal}\) yields the lax-monoidal structure; no
496	  new diagram chase is needed beyond transporting the sectionwise coherence.
497	  The composite \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) is then lax monoidal by
498	  \(\mathtt{Functor.LaxMonoidal.comp}\) together with the monoidality of
499	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}_0\mathtt{OfCommRingCat}\) (Mathlib), modulo a defeq

503	  category requires \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\)-valued presheaves of rings, so the

505	  the project this holds: the relevant \(\varphi\) is
506	  \((\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\), which is
507	  structure-sheaf-valued and hence commutative-ring-valued.

510	\begin{lemma}
511	
512	\leanok
513	[Tensor product commutes with restriction along an open immersion]
514	  \label{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_restrict_iso}
516	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
517	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be
518	  \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules, and let
519	  \(f : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion, with \((-)|_f\) the
520	  restriction of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along \(f\). The canonical comparison
521	  morphism
522	  \[
523	    (M \otimes_X N)\big|_f
524	      \;\xrightarrow{\;\;}\;
525	    M\big|_f \;\otimes_U\; N\big|_f
526	  \]
527	  is an isomorphism. No local-freeness, flatness, or line-bundle hypothesis on
528	  \(M, N\) is required: the statement holds for all \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.
529	  The reason is structural to open immersions --- restriction along \(f\) is
530	  base change along the structure-sheaf \emph{isomorphism} induced by \(f\),
531	  not along a general ring map --- and base change along a ring isomorphism is
532	  trivially invertible and commutes with tensor products.
533	\end{lemma}
534	
535	\begin{proof}
536	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
537	  Write \(\varphi\) for the morphism of structure-sheaf-valued presheaves of
538	  rings induced by the open immersion \(f\) (concretely
539	  \(\varphi = (\mathtt{Scheme.Hom.toRingCatSheafHom}\,f).\mathrm{hom}\)), so that
540	  restriction along \(f\) is computed by the inverse-image (pullback) functor on
541	  presheaves of modules. The proof exploits the defining feature of an open
542	  immersion: the comparison of structure-sheaf rings over the image is an
543	  \emph{isomorphism}, not merely a ring map. For an open \(V \subseteq U\) the
544	  section ring \(\Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr)\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) over the image
545	  of \(V\) is identified with \(\Gamma(U, V)\) by the canonical \emph{ring
546	  isomorphism}
547	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\,V : \Gamma\bigl(X, f(V)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\sim}
548	  \Gamma(U, V)\)
549	  (the \(\mathtt{CommRingCat}\) isomorphism
550	  \(\mathtt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appIso}\)). Restriction along \(f\) is
551	  therefore extension of scalars along
552	  this ring isomorphism, and the proof needs no flatness anywhere.
553	
554	  \emph{Step 1 (reduce restriction to the abstract pullback).} The restriction
555	  \((-)|_f\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules along the open immersion \(f\) is
556	  naturally isomorphic to the abstract presheaf-of-modules pullback
557	  \((\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(-)\), via the
558	  present comparison \(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\). It
559	  therefore suffices to produce the comparison and prove it an isomorphism for
560	  the abstract pullback functor.
561	
562	  \emph{Step 2 (move the pullback inside the sheafification).} The substrate
563	  \(\otimes_X\) is sheafification of the presheaf-of-modules tensor product
564	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}). The pullback commutes with
565	  sheafification: there is a natural isomorphism
566	  \(\mathtt{sheafification} \circ \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
567	   \;\cong\;
568	   \mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi \circ \mathtt{sheafification}\)
569	  (the present \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.sheafificationCompPullback}\)). Hence the
570	  whole question descends to the presheaf level, where it is the base-change
571	  comparison
572	  \[
573	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}(A \otimes B)
574	      \;\longrightarrow\;
575	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,A
576	      \;\otimes\;
577	    (\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi).\mathrm{obj}\,B
578	  \]
579	  for the underlying presheaves of modules \(A, B\) of \(M\) and \(N\).
580	
581	  \emph{Step 3 (the genuine residual: H1 alone).} Completing this comparison at the
582	  presheaf level requires a sectionwise handle on the abstract functor
583	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.pullback}\,\varphi\), which is defined as the left
584	  adjoint of pushforward and admits no sectionwise formula in Mathlib at the pinned
585	  commit. The closure passes through two presheaf-level ingredients, of which
586	  exactly one now remains open.
587	
588	  The first ingredient --- a strong-monoidal upgrade of
589	  \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}\) along the structure-sheaf ring \emph{isomorphism}
590	  \(f.\mathtt{appIso}\), so that base change along the isomorphism commutes with
591	  \(\otimes\) --- is \emph{closed in this chapter} as
592	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}
593	  (\(\mathtt{restrictScalarsRingIsoTensorEquiv}\)): for a ring isomorphism
594	  \(e : R \simeq S\) the canonical map \(a \otimes_R b \mapsto a \otimes_S b\) is an
595	  \(R\)-linear equivalence \(\mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M) \otimes_R
596	  \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(N) \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathtt{restrictScalars}_e(M
597	  \otimes_S N)\). This was the former ``H2'' bottom gap.
598	
599	  The \emph{sole remaining residual} is therefore the presheaf-level identification
600	  \[
601	    \mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \;\cong\; \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi
602	  \]
603	  via \(\mathtt{Adjunction.leftAdjointUniq}\) (``H1''). One exhibits a
604	  presheaf-level adjunction
605	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \dashv \mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\) and concludes
606	  that \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta\), being a left adjoint of
607	  \(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\varphi\), is canonically isomorphic to the abstract left
608	  adjoint \(\mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\). Here \(\beta\) is the structure map of the
609	  restriction functor, so that \((M|_f).\mathrm{val} =
610	  (\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta).\mathrm{obj}\,M.\mathrm{val}\) definitionally;
611	  composing the resulting comparison with
612	  \cref{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv} yields the asserted isomorphism.
613	
614	  This presheaf-level adjunction is the presheaf analogue of the sheaf-level
615	  \(\mathtt{SheafOfModules.pushforwardPushforwardAdj}\), and must be built from
616	  presheaf-level \(\mathtt{pushforwardNatTrans}\) and
617	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardCongr}\). These two building blocks are \emph{Mathlib-absent}
618	  at the pinned commit: only the \emph{sheaf}-level versions exist
619	  (\texttt{Mathlib/.../SheafOfModules/Sheaf/PushforwardContinuous.lean}), whereas
620	  \(\mathtt{pushforwardId}\) and \(\mathtt{pushforwardComp}\) do already exist at the
621	  presheaf level. The depth of this sub-chain is \emph{uncertain} and may span
622	  several iterations.
623	
624	  This lemma is the single substrate linchpin of the relative Picard group law: the
625	  direct gluing construction of the associator \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso}
626	  invokes it (twice per cover member) to descend to the canonical presheaf-level
627	  associator. Closing the H1 residual is therefore the sole remaining substrate
628	  obligation.
629	
630	  \emph{The make-or-break: only the free-cover case is on the critical path.} The
631	  statement above is proved here for \emph{arbitrary} \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
632	  \(M, N\), and in that generality the closure passes through the H1 residual
633	  (\(\mathtt{pushforward}\,\beta \cong \mathtt{pullback}\,\varphi\)), whose

642	  general presheaf-pushforward adjunction. The PRIMARY proof obligation of the

655	  \label{lem:restrictscalars_ringiso_tensorequiv}

758	  By the locally-bijective characterisation of the sheafification localizer

766	  \(R(U)\)-linear map

1056	  isomorphism. The presheaf-of-modules tensor is sectionwise, so it commutes with
1057	  the stalk (a filtered colimit), giving
1058	  \[
1059	    (F \triangleleft g)_x
1060	      \;=\; \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes_{R_x} g_x
1061	      \;\colon\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} M_x \;\longrightarrow\; F_x \otimes_{R_x} N_x .
1062	  \]
1063	  Tensoring an isomorphism \(g_x\) by the identity \(\mathrm{id}_{F_x}\) yields an
1064	  isomorphism --- no flatness is needed, since \(g_x\) is invertible, not merely
1065	  injective. Hence \(F \triangleleft g\) is a stalkwise isomorphism, in particular
1066	  locally bijective, in particular locally injective, for \emph{any} \(F\).
1067	
1068	  The stalkwise computation rests on three ingredients with distinct status at the
1069	  pinned commit:
1070	  \begin{enumerate}
1071	    \item[(d.1-done)] the \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear stalk-map packaging
1072	      (\(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap}\) and its companions, \cref{lem:stalk_linear_map}):
1073	      a morphism of presheaves of modules induces an \(R.\mathtt{stalk}\,x\)-linear
1074	      map on stalks, characterised on germs, that becomes a linear equivalence once
1075	      the underlying \(\mathtt{Ab}\)-stalk map is an isomorphism. This is built
1076	      project-side, axiom-clean, and furnishes the \(\mathrm{id} \otimes g_x\) step
1077	      above.
1078	    \item[(d.1-bridge)] (remaining) the site-\(J.W\) \(\Longleftrightarrow\)
1079	      stalkwise-iso characterisation on \(\mathtt{Opens}\,X\): that a morphism lies
1080	      in \(J.W\) iff its stalk maps are isomorphisms at every point. The concrete
1081	      Mathlib assembly route uses the \(\mathtt{WEqualsLocallyBijective}\) structure
1082	      together with the topological stalk criteria --- local injectivity
1083	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) injectivity on stalks and local surjectivity
1084	      \(\Longleftrightarrow\) surjectivity on stalks --- bridging the site-level
1085	      \(\mathtt{Presheaf.IsLocallyInjective}\) / \(\mathtt{IsLocallySurjective}\)
1086	      to the \(\mathtt{TopCat.Presheaf}\) stalk versions. This half is still
1087	      Mathlib-absent at the \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) level.
1088	    \item[(d.2)] (remaining) the commutation \((A \otimes^{p} B)_x \cong A_x
1089	      \otimes_{R_x} B_x\) of the stalk (a filtered colimit) with the relative
1090	      module-presheaf tensor, under which \((F \triangleleft g)_x\) is identified
1091	      with \(\mathrm{lTensor}\,F_x\,(g_x) = \mathrm{id}_{F_x} \otimes g_x\) (using
1092	      that \(\mathtt{tensorLeft}\) / \(\mathtt{tensorRight}\) preserve filtered
1093	      colimits over a module category). This is genuinely Mathlib-absent over a
1094	      \emph{varying} ring and is the largest remaining piece.
1095	  \end{enumerate}
1096	  Mathlib supplies the analogues for presheaves valued in a fixed monoidal concrete
1097	  category, but not for modules over a varying ring; porting (d.1-bridge) and (d.2)
1098	  to \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\) is the remaining work. Once (d.2) lands, the
1099	  conclusion is immediate and flatness-free: by
1100	  \(\mathtt{stalkLinearMap\_bijective\_of\_isIso}\) the stalk map \(g_x\) is a
1101	  linear equivalence, and the linear-equivalence \(\mathrm{lTensor}\) by \(F_x\)
1102	  carries it to an isomorphism, finishing the proof.

1105	  \(J.W.\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\) / \(\mathtt{LocalizedMonoidal}\) route~(e) --- the
1106	  monoidal category on \emph{all} of \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), for an
1107	  arbitrary whisker object \(F\) --- is later wanted. For the relative Picard group
1108	  law the whisker objects are locally trivial, and the PRIMARY route above suffices,
1109	  trading the Mathlib-absent (d.2) for the single comparison
1110	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}.

1231	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
1232	  \(M, N, P \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). There is an isomorphism
1233	  \[
1234	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
1235	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1236	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
1237	  \]
1238	  in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), natural in \(M, N, P\). The group law on
1239	  \(\otimes\)-iso-classes (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}) consumes only
1240	  the \emph{existence} of this isomorphism, the proposition
1241	  \(\mathrm{Nonempty}\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \cong M \otimes_X (N
1242	  \otimes_X P)\bigr)\); no pentagon, triangle, or naturality coherence of it is
1243	  ever required.

1245	  The current Lean pin additionally carries \emph{locally trivial}
1246	  (\(\mathtt{LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}\), \cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial})
1247	  hypotheses on \(M, N, P\). The construction below produces the isomorphism for

1251	
1252	\begin{proof}
1253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}
1254	  The isomorphism is assembled by \emph{gluing canonical local isomorphisms}. The
1255	  construction rests on \emph{no} whiskering of the sheafification localizer,
1256	  \emph{no} instance \((J.W).\mathtt{IsMonoidal}\), and \emph{no} stalk
1257	  computation.
1258	
1259	  Fix a common open cover \(\{U\}\) of \(X\) (for instance an affine open cover).
1260	  On each member \(U\) form the canonical composite
1261	  \[
1262	    \bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P\bigr)\big|_U
1263	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1264	    \bigl(M|_U \otimes_U N|_U\bigr) \otimes_U P|_U
1265	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1266	    M|_U \otimes_U \bigl(N|_U \otimes_U P|_U\bigr)
1267	      \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1268	    \bigl(M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\bigr)\big|_U ,
1269	  \]
1270	  in which:
1271	  \begin{itemize}
1272	    \item the first arrow is two applications of the restriction-compatibility
1273	      isomorphism \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} along the open immersion
1274	      \(U \hookrightarrow X\): first \((M \otimes_X N)|_U \cong M|_U \otimes_U
1275	      N|_U\) tensored with \(P|_U\), then \(\bigl((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X
1276	      P\bigr)|_U \cong (M \otimes_X N)|_U \otimes_U P|_U\);
1277	    \item the middle arrow is the \emph{canonical presheaf-level associator}
1278	      \(\alpha\) of the (symmetric) monoidal category
1279	      \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,\mathcal{O}_U\)
1280	      (\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.monoidalCategoryStruct.associator}\),
1281	      \cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)), read on the restricted modules;
1282	    \item the third arrow is again two applications of
1283	      \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}, in the opposite direction.
1284	  \end{itemize}
1285	  Each of the three arrows is a \emph{canonical} isomorphism: the
1286	  restriction-compatibility comparison \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} is induced
1287	  by the open immersion with no choices, hence natural in its module arguments, and
1288	  the presheaf associator \(\alpha\) is a fixed natural transformation. Therefore,
1289	  on a double overlap \(U \cap U'\), restricting the \(U\)-composite and the
1290	  \(U'\)-composite to \(U \cap U'\) yields the same morphism: each of the three
1291	  arrows commutes with the further restriction to \(U \cap U'\) by the naturality of
1292	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso} and of the presheaf associator, so the two local
1293	  composites agree on the overlap.
1294	
1295	  Because the internal hom \(\mathcal{H}om(-,-)\) of sheaves of
1296	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is itself a sheaf on \(X\), a family of morphisms defined
1297	  on the members of an open cover and agreeing on the overlaps glues to a single
1298	  global morphism. Applying this both to the local composites above and to their
1299	  local inverses produces a global morphism
1300	  \((M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P \to M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)\) whose restriction
1301	  to each \(U\) is the local composite, together with a two-sided inverse glued from
1302	  the local inverses; hence the glued morphism is a global isomorphism --- the
1303	  associator.
1304	
1305	  The single non-formal input is the restriction-compatibility comparison
1306	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_restrict_iso}; the presheaf associator
1307	  (\cref{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}(i)) and the sheaf-of-modules internal-hom gluing
1308	  are already available. Note that, unlike the pointwise-existence statement
1309	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial} --- a \emph{proposition} proved
1310	  cover-pointwise with \emph{no} overlap obligation --- the associator is
1311	  \emph{global data}, a single isomorphism of sheaves on all of \(X\). Assembling it
1312	  from local pieces is therefore genuinely stronger than that pattern: the
1313	  overlap-naturality compatibility of the local composites must be discharged before
1314	  gluing, and is discharged above. The locally-trivial hypotheses of the statement
1315	  are not consumed.
1316	\end{proof}

1821	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
1822	  % topologies  by
1823	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
1824	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
1825	  %  \end{dfn}"
1826	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
1827	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
1828	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
1829	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
1830	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
1831	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
1832	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
1833	  \[
1834	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
1835	  \]
1836	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
1837	  abelian-group structure with addition
1838	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
1839	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
1840	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
1841	  inverse
1842	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
1843	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
1844	  supplied by the substrate of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
1845	  restricted to the \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier
1846	  via \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
1847	  map
1848	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
1849	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
1850	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1851	\end{theorem}
1852	
1853	\begin{proof}
1854	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
1855	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
1856	        def:scheme_modules_isinvertible,
1857	        lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup,
1858	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
1859	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
1860	  Write \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the set of isomorphism classes of objects
1861	  of \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) (line bundles on
1862	  \(C \times_k T\)), with the operation
1863	  \([L] + [L'] := [L \otimes_{C \times_k T} L']\). This operation is
1864	  well-defined on isomorphism classes: an isomorphism \(L \cong L_0\) tensors
1865	  to an isomorphism \(L \otimes L' \cong L_0 \otimes L'\) by functoriality of
1866	  \(\otimes\) (\cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}), and
1867	  symmetrically in the second argument. The abelian-group structure on
1868	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is then exactly the \emph{units group of the
1869	  commutative monoid of \(\otimes\)-iso-classes}
1870	  (\cref{lem:tensorobj_isoclass_commgroup}): multiplication
1871	  \([L]\cdot[L'] = [L \otimes_{C\times_k T} L']\) is well-defined and
1872	  commutative-monoidal by the coherence isomorphisms
1873	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_assoc_iso,lem:tensorobj_unit_iso,lem:tensorobj_comm_iso},
1874	  and an iso-class is a unit precisely when its carrier is
1875	  \(\otimes\)-invertible (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the inverse
1876	  being carried by the predicate. Each axiom is a \emph{proposition} --- the
1877	  existence of an isomorphism --- so no monoidal-coherence datum (pentagon,
1878	  triangle, hexagon) is invoked; the \(\AddCommGroup\) on
1879	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the additive form of this units group. This mirrors
1880	  Mathlib's own Picard group: \(\mathtt{CommRing.Pic}\,R\) carries its
1881	  \(\mathtt{CommGroup}\) (\(\mathtt{instCommGroupPic}\),
1882	  \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup}) transported from
1883	  \(\mathrm{Units}\bigl(\mathrm{Skeleton}(\mathtt{ModuleCat}\,R)\bigr)\); here
1884	  the same \(\mathrm{Units}(\cdots)\) shape is transported to the scheme-level
1885	  \(\otimes_X\). (The scheme-level Picard group is genuinely absent from
1886	  Mathlib --- \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.PicardGroup} records ``connect to
1887	  invertible sheaves'' as an open task --- so the project supplies it lightly.)
1888	  The invertibility-predicate carrier is the project's \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\)
1889	  (\cref{def:scheme_modules_isinvertible}), the scheme-level analogue of
1890	  Mathlib's \(\mathtt{Module.Invertible}\); the geometric predicate
1891	  \(\mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\) is retained separately and is connected to
1892	  \(\mathtt{IsInvertible}\) only by an off-critical-path equivalence (only the
1893	  geometric \(\mathtt{IsInvertible} \Leftarrow \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\)-style
1894	  direction is nontrivial, and it is not needed here).
1895	
1896	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback functor
1897	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
1898	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carries tensor products to
1899	  tensor products and the unit to the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism
1900	  classes \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
1901	  homomorphism, and its image is a subgroup.
1902	
1903	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set of
1904	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of cosets of this subgroup. The
1905	  quotient of an abelian group by a subgroup is itself an abelian group, via
1906	  the standard \(\mathtt{QuotientAddGroup}\) construction, and the quotient
1907	  map is a surjective group homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup
1908	  (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the general
1909	  quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra textbook). Putting
1910	  the pieces together gives the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on
1911	  \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the statement, and identifies it with the
1912	  structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
1913	\end{proof}

/-!
# The Picard group of a commutative ring

This file defines the Picard group `CommRing.Pic R` of a commutative ring `R` as the type of
invertible `R`-modules (in the sense that `M` is invertible if there exists another `R`-module
`N` such that `M ⊗[R] N ≃ₗ[R] R`) up to isomorphism, equipped with tensor product as multiplication.

## Main definition

- `Module.Invertible R M` says that the canonical map `Mᵛ ⊗[R] M → R` is an isomorphism.
  To show that `M` is invertible, it suffices to provide an arbitrary `R`-module `N`
  and an isomorphism `N ⊗[R] M ≃ₗ[R] R`, see `Module.Invertible.right`.

- `ClassGroup.equivPic`: the class group of a domain is isomorphic to the Picard group.

## Main results

- An invertible module is finite and projective (provided as instances).

- `Module.Invertible.free_iff_linearEquiv`: an invertible module is free iff it is isomorphic to
  the ring, i.e. its class is trivial in the Picard group.

- `Submodule.ker_unitsToPic`, `Submodule.range_unitsToPic`: exactness of the sequence
  `1 → Rˣ → Aˣ → (Submodule R A)ˣ → Pic R → Pic A` at the last two spots.
  See Theorem 2.4 in [RobertsSingh1993] or Exercise I.3.7(iv) and Proposition I.3.5 in [Weibel2013].

## References

- https://qchu.wordpress.com/2014/10/19/the-picard-groups/
- https://mathoverflow.net/questions/13768/what-is-the-right-definition-of-the-picard-group-of-a-commutative-ring
- https://mathoverflow.net/questions/375725/picard-group-vs-class-group
- [Weibel2013], https://sites.math.rutgers.edu/~weibel/Kbook/Kbook.I.pdf
- [Stacks: Picard groups of rings](https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0AFW)

## TODO

  See [Stacks: Finite projective modules](https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/00NX).
- Connect to invertible sheaves on `Spec R`. More generally, connect projective `R`-modules of
  constant finite rank to locally free sheaves on `Spec R`.
- Exhibit isomorphism with sheaf cohomology `H¹(Spec R, 𝓞ˣ)`.
-/
